claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 9 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 9 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances. Let me start by gathering some information.

Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child – my son Alex. He's 9 years old. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I can see you have one child, Alex, who is 9 years old.

To properly assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to read the official guidance first. Let me do that now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. I've reviewed the eligibility rules. Now I need to ask you a few questions to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

First question: Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't live in the UK. I'm living abroad at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since you're living abroad, there are specific rules that apply to your situation.

Could you tell me which country you're currently living in? Also, are you or have you been a Crown servant (such as a diplomat or member of the armed forces posted abroad)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm not a Crown servant - I'm not a diplomat or in the armed forces or anything like that. I'd rather not say exactly which country I'm in, but it's not one where I'd qualify under those special arrangements. I'm just living abroad normally.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I understand you're living abroad and you're not a Crown servant.

Now I need to ask about your son Alex. Does Alex live with you abroad, or does he live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex doesn't live with me. He's back in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. So Alex is living in the UK while you're living abroad.

Could you tell me who Alex is living with in the UK? For example, is he living with another parent, a relative, or someone else?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's living with his other parent - my ex-partner.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining that. So Alex is living with your ex-partner in the UK.

Now, an important question: Do you contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep? For example, do you send money for things like food, clothes, pocket money, or contribute towards his accommodation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute towards his upkeep. I send money regularly to help with things like clothes, food, and other things he needs.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. It's helpful to know you're contributing towards Alex's upkeep.

Could you tell me approximately how much you contribute each month, and is your ex-partner currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I contribute £30 a week, so that's about £120 a month. As for whether my ex-partner is claiming Child Benefit - I'm not sure, actually. They might be, but I don't know for certain.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. You're contributing £30 per week (approximately £120 per month) towards Alex's upkeep.

Based on the rules I've reviewed, I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me explain the situation:

According to the Child Benefit rules, you normally need to live in the UK to qualify for Child Benefit. There are exceptions for people living abroad in certain countries or if you're a Crown servant, but you've confirmed that neither of those exceptions apply to you.

Additionally, the rules state that you'll usually be responsible for a child if either you live with them OR you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them. The current Child Benefit rate for the eldest or only child is £25.60 per week. You're contributing £30 per week, which exceeds this amount.

However, the fundamental requirement is that you must live in the UK to qualify for Child Benefit, unless specific exceptions apply. Since you're living abroad and don't meet any of the exceptions (such as being a Crown servant or living in a country with a reciprocal agreement), and Alex is living in the UK with his other parent, you would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

It's also important to note that only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at any time. If your ex-partner is living with Alex in the UK, they would be the person who could claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Assessment Complete**

I'm sorry, but based on your circumstances, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why:

The main requirement for Child Benefit is that you must live in the UK. Since you're living abroad and you're not a Crown servant (such as a diplomat or armed forces member), and you're not living in a country with special reciprocal arrangements, you don't meet this fundamental requirement.

While it's positive that you're contributing £30 per week towards Alex's upkeep (which is more than the current Child Benefit rate), this alone doesn't overcome the requirement to live in the UK.

Additionally, since Alex lives in the UK with his other parent, and only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child, his other parent would be the appropriate person to make a claim for Child Benefit for Alex, as they live with him in the UK.

If you have any other questions about this decision, please feel free to ask.

reasonings: Alex is 9 years old (under 16), so meets the age requirement. The claimant contributes £30 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit rate of £25.60 per week. However, the claimant does not live in the UK and is living abroad. The claimant is not a Crown servant and does not live in a country where special arrangements would allow them to claim Child Benefit. According to the eligibility rules, you normally must live in the UK to qualify for Child Benefit unless specific exceptions apply (such as being a Crown servant or living in certain countries with reciprocal agreements). Since the claimant does not meet any of these exceptions and does not live in the UK, they are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. Additionally, Alex lives with his other parent in the UK, and only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child - the other parent would be the appropriate claimant as they live with Alex in the UK.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is living abroad (not in the UK) and is not a Crown servant or living in a country with special arrangements. Their 9-year-old son Alex lives in the UK with his other parent. Although the claimant contributes £30 per week towards Alex's upkeep (which exceeds the Child Benefit amount), the fundamental requirement to live in the UK is not met and no exceptions apply. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit. The other parent living with Alex in the UK would be the appropriate person to claim.

Since the claimant does not meet any of these exceptions and does not live in the UK, they are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. Additionally, Alex lives with his other parent in the UK, and only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child - the other parent would be the appropriate claimant as they live with Alex in the UK.

Their 9-year-old son Alex lives in the UK with his other parent. Although the claimant contributes £30 per week towards Alex's upkeep (which exceeds the Child Benefit amount), the fundamental requirement to live in the UK is not met and no exceptions apply. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit. The other parent living with Alex in the UK would be the appropriate person to claim.
